state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Water quality monitoring IoT system with pH, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, and conductivity sensors. Real-time alerting, trend analysis, and EPA compliance reporting.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user stories, and ROI model for the water quality monitoring system. Identify stakeholders (municipal utilities, environmental agencies, industrial facilities), document regulatory obligations (EPA Clean Water Act thresholds), and map current manual monitoring workflows to eliminate.
acceptance_criteria: Stakeholder map covers all primary and secondary users with RACI
User stories written for sensor calibration, alert management, EPA report generation, and trend dashboards
ROI model shows cost-per-sample reduction vs manual lab testing
Process flow diagrams cover normal operation, alert escalation, and compliance reporting cycles
Integration points with existing systems mapped
Migration plan drafted

plan.description: Write the Product Requirements Document (PRD) for water_quality_monitor. Define MVP scope (4 sensor types, real-time alerting, EPA report export), V2 features (predictive anomaly detection, multi-site management), success metrics (alert latency <30s, report generation <60s, 99.5% uptime), and a 12-month roadmap.
acceptance_criteria: PRD reviewed and signed off by business_analyst and ux_designer
MVP vs V2 feature boundary clearly documented with rationale
All KPIs have baseline targets and measurement methods defined
Roadmap aligns with EPA reporting calendar (quarterly/annual cycles)

plan.description: Analyze the water quality monitoring market: competitor landscape (YSI, Hach, In-Situ), pricing models (hardware + SaaS vs perpetual license), regulatory tailwinds (EPA PFAS rules, SDWA amendments), and positioning strategy for water_quality_monitor as a cloud-native, open-protocol alternative.
acceptance_criteria: Competitor feature-price matrix completed for top 5 vendors
Differentiation narrative (3 key points) documented
Market sizing estimate with TAM/SAM/SOM for municipal and industrial segments
Go-to-market channel recommendations documented

plan.description: Conduct user research with water utility operators and environmental compliance officers. Design wireframes for: real-time sensor dashboard, alert management inbox, trend analysis charts, EPA report builder, and sensor calibration wizard. Define accessibility requirements (WCAG 2.1 AA) and mobile-responsive breakpoints.
acceptance_criteria: User research sessions completed with at least 3 operators and 2 compliance officers
Wireframes produced for all 6 core pages with annotated interaction flows
Mobile-responsive layout defined for dashboard and alert views
Calibration wizard covers 4-point calibration for all sensor types

plan.description: Produce a threat model for the IoT sensor network and cloud platform. Cover device identity (X.509 certificates), firmware OTA security, TLS 1.3 for sensor-to-cloud transport, API authentication (OAuth2 + JWT), data-at-rest encryption (AES-256), and IEC 62443 zone/conduit model. Generate initial SBOM for firmware and backend dependencies.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model covers all 5 attack surfaces: device, transport, cloud API, web UI, admin console
IEC 62443 security levels assigned to each zone (field devices SL-2, cloud platform SL-3)
SBOM generated listing all firmware and backend dependencies with CVE scan results
Mitigations documented for all HIGH and CRITICAL threats
Penetration test plan scoped and scheduled
Device identity provisioned
Firmware signing enabled

plan.description: Map product requirements to IEC 62443, ISO 27001, and SOC 2 Type II controls. Produce compliance matrix tracing each control to implementation evidence artifacts. Define EPA reporting data retention policy (7 years), audit log requirements, and chain-of-custody for sensor calibration records.
acceptance_criteria: Compliance matrix maps each IEC 62443, ISO 27001 Annex A, and SOC 2 TSC control to a product feature or process
EPA 40 CFR Part 136 method references documented for each parameter
Data retention and deletion schedule documented and implemented in architecture
Calibration chain-of-custody workflow documented with electronic signature requirements

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy (GDPR + CCPA for location and monitoring data), and open-source license review for all third-party firmware and backend libraries. Review EPA data submission agreements and any state-level environmental reporting requirements.
acceptance_criteria: Terms of Service covers SLA commitments, data ownership, and liability limitations
Privacy Policy addresses all sensor data and location data collection practices
OSS license scan completed with no GPL-incompatible licenses in commercial distribution
EPA data sharing agreements reviewed and data format compliance confirmed

plan.description: Bootstrap the water_quality_monitor SAGE solution from the starter template. Configure project.yaml (solution metadata, active_modules, theme), prompts.yaml (analyst, developer, compliance specialist roles with water-quality domain context), and tasks.yaml (sensor_calibration, alert_triage, epa_report_generation, trend_analysis task types). Set up the multi-agent coordinator pattern for orchestrating firmware, backend, and compliance agents.
acceptance_criteria: project.yaml validates against SAGE schema with all required fields populated
prompts.yaml defines analyst, developer, and regulatory_specialist roles with water-quality system prompts
tasks.yaml registers all 5 custom task types with descriptions and required fields
Solution loads cleanly via `make run PROJECT=water_quality_monitor` with no import errors
Coordinator agent pattern configured to route firmware tasks to developer, compliance tasks to regulatory_specialist

plan.description: Design and implement the time-series database schema for sensor readings. Use TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension) with hypertables partitioned by time. Tables: sensor_nodes, readings (pH, turbidity, dissolved_oxygen, conductivity, timestamp, node_id), alert_events, calibration_records, epa_report_snapshots. Implement data retention policies and continuous aggregates for hourly/daily rollups.
acceptance_criteria: All hypertables created with correct partitioning and compression policies
Continuous aggregates refresh automatically on new data insertion
Retention policy enforced: raw data compressed after 30 days, deleted after 7 years
Write throughput benchmarked at >10,000 readings/second for 1,000-node deployments
Schema migrations versioned with Alembic

plan.description: Implement embedded firmware for the sensor node (ESP32-S3 or STM32U5 microcontroller). HAL drivers for: Atlas Scientific pH EZO circuit (UART), Dfrobot Turbidity sensor (analog ADC), Atlas Scientific DO EZO (UART), Atlas Scientific EC EZO (UART/I2C). FreeRTOS tasks: sensor_sample_task (1Hz), connectivity_task (MQTT over TLS), calibration_task (on-demand), OTA_update_task. Implement circular buffer for offline data buffering (24h at 1Hz).
acceptance_criteria: All 4 sensor HAL drivers implemented with error detection and retry logic
FreeRTOS task priorities set: connectivity > sensor_sample > calibration > OTA
Offline circular buffer stores 86,400 readings and replays on reconnect with no data loss
OTA update validated: firmware signature verified before applying, rollback on boot failure
Power consumption measured: <150mA active, <5mA deep sleep
Firmware compiles cleanly for ESP32-S3 target with zero warnings at -Wall
OTA update mechanism implemented
Watchdog timer configured

plan.description: Build the cloud backend service (FastAPI + Python). Endpoints: MQTT broker bridge (EMQX) → ingestion pipeline → TimescaleDB. REST API: sensor nodes CRUD, readings query (with time range, aggregation), alert management, calibration record submission, EPA report generation (PDF + JSON), trend analysis (rolling averages, threshold breach counts). Implement the multi-agent coordinator for automated anomaly detection and alert triage using SAGE agent framework.
acceptance_criteria: MQTT ingestion pipeline handles 10,000 msg/s with <100ms end-to-end latency
Alert engine evaluates all threshold rules within 5 seconds of reading arrival
EPA report endpoint generates a compliant PDF report in <60 seconds for 90-day dataset
Trend analysis endpoint returns 7/30/90-day rolling averages with anomaly flags
All endpoints authenticated via OAuth2 JWT; unauthorized requests return 401
OpenAPI spec auto-generated and matches all implemented endpoints

plan.description: Implement the AI anomaly detection and alert triage agent using the multi-agent coordinator pattern. Coordinator routes incoming sensor anomalies to: (1) Analyst Agent — performs root cause analysis using historical patterns and vector memory, (2) Alert Triage Agent — classifies severity and recommends response, (3) Compliance Agent — checks if reading breaches EPA thresholds and triggers report generation. All proposals go through HITL approval gate before escalation actions.
acceptance_criteria: Coordinator correctly routes anomaly events to all 3 specialist agents in parallel
Analyst agent retrieves prior similar anomalies from vector memory within 2 seconds
EPA threshold check produces a structured proposal with violation details and recommended action
All escalation and report-generation proposals enter HITL approval queue — none auto-execute
Feedback loop: operator approval/rejection stored in vector memory for future context retrieval

plan.description: Build the React 18 + TypeScript dashboard for water_quality_monitor following SAGE UI patterns. Pages: real-time sensor dashboard (4 gauge cards + sparklines), alert inbox (sortable by severity/time), trend analysis (recharts line charts with EPA threshold overlays), EPA report builder (date picker + parameter selector + PDF export), sensor node management, calibration wizard (step-by-step 4-point calibration UI). Use SAGE ThemeProvider with water_quality_monitor theme colors.
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard displays live readings for all 4 parameters with color-coded threshold status (green/yellow/red)
Alert inbox supports filter by severity, sensor node, and date range; bulk acknowledge works
Trend charts render 90-day history in <2s with EPA threshold reference lines overlaid
EPA report builder validates date range and selected parameters before generating PDF
Calibration wizard completes full 4-point pH calibration in <10 clicks
All pages pass WCAG 2.1 AA automated audit with zero critical violations
Mobile layout renders correctly at 375px viewport for dashboard and alerts

plan.description: Provision cloud infrastructure using Terraform. Components: Kubernetes cluster (EKS/GKE) for backend + EMQX broker, TimescaleDB on RDS, S3/GCS for report storage, CloudFront/CDN for frontend, VPC with IEC 62443 zone segmentation (OT network zone for MQTT, IT zone for API, DMZ for web), WAF rules, DDoS protection. Define IaC for all environments (dev, staging, prod).
acceptance_criteria: All infrastructure defined as Terraform modules with no manual console steps
VPC zone segmentation enforced: MQTT broker in OT zone, API in IT zone, no direct OT→internet path
WAF rules block OWASP Top 10 attack patterns on all public endpoints
RDS TimescaleDB automated backups every 6 hours with 30-day retention
DR runbook tested: RTO achieved in <60 minutes, RPO data loss <15 minutes
Cost estimate for 1,000-node deployment documented in infra/COST_ESTIMATE.md

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines for firmware (PlatformIO + GitHub Actions), backend (pytest + Docker build + Kubernetes deploy), and frontend (Vite build + Playwright E2E + CDN deploy). Add monitoring stack: Prometheus + Grafana dashboards for MQTT throughput, ingestion latency, alert engine lag, and sensor uptime. PagerDuty integration for P0/P1 alerts. SRE runbooks for common failure modes.
acceptance_criteria: Firmware CI pipeline builds and flashes to hardware emulator on every PR
Backend CI runs full pytest suite and blocks merge on any test failure
Blue/green deployment achieves zero-downtime deploys for backend
Grafana dashboard shows MQTT throughput, p99 ingestion latency, and alert engine lag with SLO burn rate
PagerDuty fires within 2 minutes of sensor ingestion pipeline failure
All 4 SRE runbooks reviewed and include rollback steps

plan.description: Write firmware unit tests (Unity framework) and HIL test specs for all 4 sensor drivers. Unit tests: HAL driver data parsing, calibration math (Nernst equation for pH, DO percent saturation), MQTT message serialization, offline buffer replay logic. HIL test harness: simulate sensor signal injection via DAC, verify RTOS task scheduling under high-load, validate OTA rollback on simulated power-cut mid-flash.
acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage ≥90% for all HAL driver and calibration math modules
pH Nernst equation unit test validates against reference values at 4 calibration points
HIL test confirms RTOS tasks maintain correct priorities under 100% CPU load simulation
OTA rollback test: firmware correctly reverts to prior version after simulated power loss at 50% flash progress
Offline buffer replay test: 86,400 buffered readings uploaded in correct chronological order with zero gaps

plan.description: Write backend unit and integration tests (pytest). Cover: MQTT ingestion pipeline, alert threshold evaluation engine, TimescaleDB continuous aggregate refresh, EPA report generation (field completeness, unit compliance), OAuth2 JWT validation, multi-tenant data isolation, and the SAGE coordinator agent routing logic.
acceptance_criteria: Backend test coverage ≥85% across all modules
Alert threshold engine test: 100 edge-case readings evaluated with correct severity classifications
EPA report test: generated PDF passes field-completeness check against EPA method requirements
Multi-tenant isolation test: tenant A cannot read tenant B readings via any API path
Coordinator agent routing test: anomaly events delivered to all 3 specialist agents within timeout
All tests run against real TimescaleDB via TestContainers — no mock database

plan.description: Design the QA test plan covering: sensor reading accuracy validation (against NIST-traceable calibration standards), alert latency SLA (≤30s end-to-end from reading to notification), report generation correctness (EPA field mapping), UI functional testing (all 6 pages), and regression test suite for firmware + backend releases.
acceptance_criteria: QA test plan documents all test cases with pass/fail criteria and traceability to PRD requirements
Sensor accuracy test cases specify acceptable tolerance ranges per EPA QA/QC procedures
Alert latency test measures end-to-end time from MQTT publish to PagerDuty/email notification
UI functional test suite covers all 6 pages with 100% coverage of primary user flows
Regression suite runs in <15 minutes in CI to enable fast feedback on each release

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system tests across the full stack: physical (or simulated) sensor nodes → MQTT → ingestion → TimescaleDB → alert engine → SAGE agents → frontend. Test scenarios: normal operation at 1Hz for 1,000 nodes, EPA threshold breach triggering alert + compliance proposal, sensor offline detection, firmware OTA update without data loss, concurrent EPA report generation for 10 users.
acceptance_criteria: 1,000-node simulation at 1Hz sustained for 24 hours with zero data loss
EPA threshold breach: alert generated and HITL proposal in approval queue within 30 seconds
Sensor offline: node marked offline in dashboard within 90 seconds of last heartbeat
OTA update: 100 nodes updated simultaneously with no reading gaps longer than 10 seconds
10 concurrent EPA report generations complete without errors or data cross-contamination
System performance: p99 API latency <200ms, MQTT ingestion p99 <100ms under full load
Load test simulates expected concurrent users
Failover scenario tested

plan.description: Write operational runbooks for water_quality_monitor. Cover: sensor node onboarding (provisioning, certificate issuance, first calibration), alert escalation procedures (P1/P2/P3 severity matrix), EPA report submission workflow, planned maintenance windows, incident response for data integrity breaches, and capacity planning model for scaling to 10,000 nodes.
acceptance_criteria: Sensor onboarding runbook enables a new node to be live in <15 minutes by a non-developer operator
Alert escalation matrix defines P1/P2/P3 criteria with notification paths and SLA response times
EPA report submission runbook includes pre-submission checklist and submission confirmation steps
Data integrity incident response runbook covers detection, containment, and regulatory notification steps
Capacity planning model shows infrastructure cost and performance projections at 1K/5K/10K nodes

plan.description: Produce user training materials for three audiences: (1) Field Operators — sensor installation, calibration, and maintenance guide; (2) Water Quality Managers — dashboard navigation, alert management, and trend interpretation guide; (3) Compliance Officers — EPA report generation, data export, and audit trail guide. Include quick-reference cards and a video script for each audience.
acceptance_criteria: Field operator guide covers unboxing, mounting, wiring, network provisioning, and 4-point calibration for all sensors
Manager guide covers all 6 dashboard pages with annotated screenshots
Compliance officer guide walks through full EPA report generation and data export workflow
Each guide reviewed by one representative user from each audience group
Video scripts timed and include screen capture call-outs
Admin guide separate from end-user guide
Role-based training paths defined

plan.description: Write technical documentation: API reference (auto-generated OpenAPI + narrative), firmware developer guide (build system, sensor driver integration, RTOS task architecture), solution architecture diagram, data model reference, and SAGE solution README for water_quality_monitor covering setup, configuration, and extension points.
acceptance_criteria: API reference covers all endpoints with request/response examples and error codes
Firmware developer guide enables a new engineer to build and flash the firmware in <30 minutes
Architecture diagram shows all components from sensor node to dashboard with data flow arrows
Solution README verified: `make run PROJECT=water_quality_monitor` works end-to-end following README steps alone
All docs reviewed by developer team for accuracy before release

flaws: Temperature compensation is entirely absent. pH, dissolved oxygen, and conductivity readings are all temperature-dependent. The EZO circuits support temperature-compensated readings (requires a DS18B20 or PT1000 probe and a separate EZO-RTD circuit), but no temperature sensor appears in the BOM, HAL driver list, schema, or calibration wizard. Without temperature compensation, every EPA report generated is scientifically invalid.

TimescaleDB on AWS RDS is not straightforward. AWS RDS for PostgreSQL supports only a limited, vetted extension list — TimescaleDB community edition requires either self-hosted EC2, Timescale Cloud, or MSB (Managed Service for TimescaleDB). The plan assumes 'RDS_TimescaleDB' as if it is a first-class RDS offering. This assumption will break infra provisioning.

Throughput acceptance criteria is internally inconsistent. Step 9 requires '>10,000 readings/second for 1,000-node deployments' but Step 10 specifies 1Hz sample rate = 1,000 msg/s for 1,000 nodes. The 10x discrepancy is never explained. Either the benchmark target is wrong or the sample rate assumption is wrong — whichever it is, the schema sizing, EMQX capacity planning, and MQTT ingestion pipeline are all built on the wrong number.

CROMERR (Cross-Media Electronic Reporting Rule, 40 CFR Part 3) is never mentioned. Any system that electronically submits compliance data to EPA or state agencies must meet CROMERR identity proofing, electronic signature, and audit trail requirements. A PDF export does not satisfy CROMERR. This is a legal blocker, not a gap — missing this means the product cannot legally file EPA reports electronically.

DFRobot SEN0189 is not suitable for EPA Method 180.1 turbidity compliance. It is a $4 analog sensor calibrated in NTU-equivalent units using scattered light at a fixed angle, with no ISO 7027 compliance and no NIST-traceable certification path. Atlas Scientific makes an EZO-TBD circuit for turbidity with a compliant probe. Using the DFRobot part invalidates the turbidity compliance claim.

Thundering herd on reconnect is unaddressed. If 1,000 nodes each hold a 24-hour circular buffer (86,400 readings) and reconnect simultaneously after a network outage, that is 86,400,000 MQTT messages arriving in a short window. The ingestion pipeline, EMQX, and TimescaleDB will be overwhelmed. There is no jitter/backoff strategy, no replay rate limiting, and no EMQX queue depth configuration to handle this scenario.

Device provisioning at scale (X.509 certificate issuance for 1,000+ nodes) is not designed. 'Device identity provisioned' appears as a one-line acceptance criterion in Step 5. In practice this requires a PKI hierarchy (root CA → intermediate CA → device cert), a zero-touch provisioning flow (device generates keypair, CSR submitted to CA at manufacturing or first boot), certificate revocation (OCSP or CRL), and rotation. None of this is designed. AWS IoT Core would solve most of it but is not in the architecture.

The ingestion pipeline between EMQX and TimescaleDB has no durability layer. The plan describes 'MQTT broker bridge (EMQX) → ingestion pipeline → TimescaleDB' with async batch inserts in FastAPI. If the FastAPI ingestion service crashes or TimescaleDB is slow, messages queued in EMQX will be dropped (QoS 0) or back-pressure will cause device disconnections. A durable message queue (Kafka, Kinesis, or even EMQX's built-in persistence) between broker and DB writer is required for the 99.5% uptime SLA with zero data loss.

MQTT QoS level is never specified anywhere in the plan. For EPA compliance and zero data loss, QoS 1 or 2 is required. QoS 0 (fire-and-forget) is the default and would silently drop readings under any network instability. This should be an explicit firmware and broker configuration requirement.

Calibration drift management is missing. The calibration wizard covers initial 4-point calibration but the plan has no: (a) calibration interval scheduling and operator reminders, (b) drift detection to flag when a sensor's response curve has shifted beyond tolerance, (c) out-of-calibration data flagging or lockout to prevent submission of stale-calibration readings to EPA reports. ISO/EPA QA/QC procedures require documented calibration intervals (typically 30–90 days for EZO circuits in field deployments).

Data quality flags are absent from the schema. EPA reporting under 40 CFR Part 136 and NPDES permits requires data qualifier flags (e.g., 'Q' for questionable, 'D' for dilution applied, 'E' for estimated, '<' for below detection limit). The readings table has no quality_flag column. Any EPA report generated without qualifier flags will be rejected by agency data systems.

Multi-site management deferred to V2 is architecturally dangerous. The database schema, API tenant isolation, and device identity model all need multi-site awareness from day 1 (foreign keys, node_id scoping, VPN segmentation per site). Retrofitting multi-site later requires schema migrations and breaking API changes. The stakeholder list includes 'municipal water utility' which universally has multiple intake/discharge points.

Step 8 (SAGE CONFIG) is a prerequisite for Step 9 (DATABASE DESIGN). This is backwards. Database schema should be designed based on domain requirements, not gated behind a YAML configuration step. The schema design for a time-series IoT system is a technical deliverable that should be independent of the agent framework configuration.

NTP time synchronization and clock drift are not addressed. Accurate timestamps are essential for EPA compliance (readings must have legally defensible timestamps). The firmware spec does not include NTP sync task, clock drift compensation, or handling of readings with invalid system time (e.g., before first NTP sync after power-on). A reading with a wrong timestamp in an EPA report is a compliance violation.
suggestions: Add a 5th sensor: Atlas Scientific EZO-RTD (temperature probe). Make temperature compensation a firmware-level requirement, not an optional calibration step. Update schema to include temperature_c column in all readings. All 3 EZO circuits (pH, DO, EC) expose temperature compensation commands — wire them together in the HAL.

Replace 'RDS_TimescaleDB' with an explicit choice: Timescale Cloud (managed, simplest), self-hosted TimescaleDB on EC2 within the same VPC (full control), or MSB. Document the chosen option in the infra architecture. If cost is a constraint, Timescale Cloud's compression reduces storage cost ~94% vs raw PostgreSQL.

Add an intermediate message bus (Amazon Kinesis Data Streams or MSK/Kafka) between EMQX and the TimescaleDB writer. This gives you replay-on-failure, back-pressure handling, and the ability to fan out the same stream to multiple consumers (ML anomaly detection, alert engine, archival) without coupling them to EMQX.

Replace the DFRobot SEN0189 with Atlas Scientific EZO-TBD + probe in the BOM and firmware spec. This keeps the sensor interface consistent (all EZO UART protocol) and provides a NIST-traceable calibration path required for EPA Method 180.1.

Add CROMERR compliance as a dedicated task between Steps 6 and 7. Define the electronic signature method (Level 2 e-signature for most NPDES submissions), identity proofing workflow for compliance officers, and the CDX (Central Data Exchange) submission format for federal EPA reporting.

Add MQTT QoS level as an explicit firmware and broker configuration requirement. Set QoS 1 for all sensor readings and QoS 2 for calibration records and alert events. Update EMQX configuration to enable persistent sessions and offline message queuing.

Add reconnect jitter and replay rate limiting to the firmware. On reconnect, each node should wait a random backoff (0–300s based on node_id hash) before replaying buffered readings, capped at a configurable max replay rate (e.g., 10 readings/second). Prevents thundering herd after mass outages.

Design the PKI and provisioning flow in Step 5 with specificity: define the CA hierarchy, the manufacturing-time provisioning flow (or first-boot provisioning via a claim certificate), and the cert rotation procedure. AWS IoT Core + Just-In-Time Provisioning would cover most of this without building custom PKI.

Add data_quality_flag (VARCHAR or ENUM) and is_temperature_compensated (BOOLEAN) columns to the readings table in Step 9. Add a view or materialized view that filters out unqualified readings for EPA report generation.

Move multi-site support to MVP scope for the schema and device identity layers only (add site_id foreign key to sensor_nodes, scope all queries by site_id). Defer the multi-site UI management to V2 as planned, but do not defer the data model change.
missing: Temperature sensor (EZO-RTD or DS18B20) in hardware BOM and temperature compensation logic in all sensor HAL drivers
CROMERR compliance design for electronic EPA submissions (40 CFR Part 3)
NPDES permit-specific report format support — most dischargers submit to state agencies via EDI or agency-specific portals, not generic PDF
PKI hierarchy design and zero-touch device provisioning workflow at manufacturing scale
Calibration interval management: scheduling, reminders, drift detection, and out-of-calibration data quarantine
Data quality flag column in schema and qualifier logic in EPA report generation
Durable message queue between EMQX and TimescaleDB writer (currently a reliability gap under the 99.5% uptime SLA)
Reconnect backoff and replay rate-limiting strategy for the 86,400-reading offline buffer
NTP sync task in firmware and timestamp validation before readings are committed to the queue
MQTT QoS level specified in firmware requirements and EMQX broker configuration
Firmware signing key management (HSM or AWS KMS for private key storage — cannot be a plaintext file in CI)
Certificate revocation mechanism (OCSP/CRL) for compromised or decommissioned devices
Sensor detection limit handling: what happens when DO = 0 or pH reads outside physical range? Schema and report generation must handle below-detection-limit values with appropriate flags
Multi-site data model (site_id) in MVP schema — cannot be retrofitted cleanly after launch
security_risks: Firmware signing key management unspecified. If the signing private key is stored in GitHub Actions secrets or as a plaintext CI environment variable, it can be exfiltrated via a compromised dependency or Actions runner. Require HSM or AWS KMS-backed signing with audit logging for every firmware release signature.

Zero-touch device provisioning gap creates a supply chain attack surface. Without a defined provisioning flow, someone inserting a rogue node with a valid claim certificate (or exploiting a permissive provisioning policy) could inject false readings into EPA compliance reports. Require per-device unique claim certificates burned at manufacturing, not shared claim certs.

EMQX topic authorization not specified. By default, EMQX permits any authenticated device to publish to any topic. A compromised sensor node could publish to other nodes' topics, injecting false readings. Require per-device ACL rules: node_id X can only publish to `sensors/{node_id}/readings`.

No mention of MQTT payload schema validation at the ingestion layer. A compromised device sending malformed JSON or oversize payloads could crash the FastAPI ingestion service or cause database errors. Validate payload schema and enforce max size at the EMQX hook layer before reaching the ingestion pipeline.

OAuth2 JWT secret rotation not addressed. If the JWT signing secret is static and leaked, all sessions are compromised with no recovery path. Require JWKS endpoint with short-lived tokens (15 min access, 24h refresh) and documented rotation procedure.

IEC 62443 zone model places MQTT broker in the OT zone with 'no direct OT→internet path', but sensors must reach the MQTT broker over the internet (or cellular) from field deployments. The zone model as described does not account for remote field devices — the OT zone would need to be a VPN tunnel termination, not a network segment. This misapplication of the zone model leaves field devices uncategorized.

No mention of rate limiting or anomaly detection on the MQTT ingestion path. A compromised node (or botnet of nodes) could flood the ingestion pipeline with high-frequency readings. Require per-device message rate limits enforced at the EMQX broker level.

SOC 2 Type II is listed as a compliance target but the timeline implies it would be in-scope from launch. SOC 2 Type II requires a minimum 6-month observation period of controls in operation. Either the launch date expectations are wrong, or the plan should target SOC 2 Type I for initial certification and Type II at the 12-month mark.
final_review.summary: This is an architecturally ambitious and well-structured plan that demonstrates genuine domain knowledge — the sensor selection, SAGE framework integration, IEC 62443 zoning intent, and TimescaleDB choice are all defensible. However, it has three categories of failure that would each independently block production: (1) Scientific invalidity — missing temperature compensation invalidates every pH, DO, and conductivity reading for EPA purposes, and the DFRobot turbidity sensor cannot satisfy Method 180.1, making the entire compliance reporting feature legally unusable; (2) Infrastructure assumptions that are wrong — TimescaleDB on RDS as described does not exist in the form assumed, and the absence of a durable message queue between EMQX and the DB writer means data loss is guaranteed under any ingestion service restart; (3) Compliance gaps — CROMERR is the legal framework for electronic EPA submissions and its absence means the product cannot file reports to federal or state agency data systems regardless of how correct the PDF looks. The plan would work as a prototype for demonstrating the architecture, but shipping it as a compliance product without addressing these issues would expose customers to regulatory liability. Score reflects strong structural foundation undermined by fundamental measurement, infrastructure, and regulatory gaps that require rework before production readiness.

flaws: Temperature compensation is entirely absent. pH, dissolved oxygen, and conductivity readings are all temperature-dependent. The EZO circuits support temperature-compensated readings (requires a DS18B20 or PT1000 probe and a separate EZO-RTD circuit), but no temperature sensor appears in the BOM, HAL driver list, schema, or calibration wizard. Without temperature compensation, every EPA report generated is scientifically invalid.

TimescaleDB on AWS RDS is not straightforward. AWS RDS for PostgreSQL supports only a limited, vetted extension list — TimescaleDB community edition requires either self-hosted EC2, Timescale Cloud, or MSB (Managed Service for TimescaleDB). The plan assumes 'RDS_TimescaleDB' as if it is a first-class RDS offering. This assumption will break infra provisioning.

Throughput acceptance criteria is internally inconsistent. Step 9 requires '>10,000 readings/second for 1,000-node deployments' but Step 10 specifies 1Hz sample rate = 1,000 msg/s for 1,000 nodes. The 10x discrepancy is never explained. Either the benchmark target is wrong or the sample rate assumption is wrong — whichever it is, the schema sizing, EMQX capacity planning, and MQTT ingestion pipeline are all built on the wrong number.

CROMERR (Cross-Media Electronic Reporting Rule, 40 CFR Part 3) is never mentioned. Any system that electronically submits compliance data to EPA or state agencies must meet CROMERR identity proofing, electronic signature, and audit trail requirements. A PDF export does not satisfy CROMERR. This is a legal blocker, not a gap — missing this means the product cannot legally file EPA reports electronically.

DFRobot SEN0189 is not suitable for EPA Method 180.1 turbidity compliance. It is a $4 analog sensor calibrated in NTU-equivalent units using scattered light at a fixed angle, with no ISO 7027 compliance and no NIST-traceable certification path. Atlas Scientific makes an EZO-TBD circuit for turbidity with a compliant probe. Using the DFRobot part invalidates the turbidity compliance claim.

Thundering herd on reconnect is unaddressed. If 1,000 nodes each hold a 24-hour circular buffer (86,400 readings) and reconnect simultaneously after a network outage, that is 86,400,000 MQTT messages arriving in a short window. The ingestion pipeline, EMQX, and TimescaleDB will be overwhelmed. There is no jitter/backoff strategy, no replay rate limiting, and no EMQX queue depth configuration to handle this scenario.

Device provisioning at scale (X.509 certificate issuance for 1,000+ nodes) is not designed. 'Device identity provisioned' appears as a one-line acceptance criterion in Step 5. In practice this requires a PKI hierarchy (root CA → intermediate CA → device cert), a zero-touch provisioning flow (device generates keypair, CSR submitted to CA at manufacturing or first boot), certificate revocation (OCSP or CRL), and rotation. None of this is designed. AWS IoT Core would solve most of it but is not in the architecture.

The ingestion pipeline between EMQX and TimescaleDB has no durability layer. The plan describes 'MQTT broker bridge (EMQX) → ingestion pipeline → TimescaleDB' with async batch inserts in FastAPI. If the FastAPI ingestion service crashes or TimescaleDB is slow, messages queued in EMQX will be dropped (QoS 0) or back-pressure will cause device disconnections. A durable message queue (Kafka, Kinesis, or even EMQX's built-in persistence) between broker and DB writer is required for the 99.5% uptime SLA with zero data loss.

MQTT QoS level is never specified anywhere in the plan. For EPA compliance and zero data loss, QoS 1 or 2 is required. QoS 0 (fire-and-forget) is the default and would silently drop readings under any network instability. This should be an explicit firmware and broker configuration requirement.

Calibration drift management is missing. The calibration wizard covers initial 4-point calibration but the plan has no: (a) calibration interval scheduling and operator reminders, (b) drift detection to flag when a sensor's response curve has shifted beyond tolerance, (c) out-of-calibration data flagging or lockout to prevent submission of stale-calibration readings to EPA reports. ISO/EPA QA/QC procedures require documented calibration intervals (typically 30–90 days for EZO circuits in field deployments).

Data quality flags are absent from the schema. EPA reporting under 40 CFR Part 136 and NPDES permits requires data qualifier flags (e.g., 'Q' for questionable, 'D' for dilution applied, 'E' for estimated, '<' for below detection limit). The readings table has no quality_flag column. Any EPA report generated without qualifier flags will be rejected by agency data systems.

Multi-site management deferred to V2 is architecturally dangerous. The database schema, API tenant isolation, and device identity model all need multi-site awareness from day 1 (foreign keys, node_id scoping, VPN segmentation per site). Retrofitting multi-site later requires schema migrations and breaking API changes. The stakeholder list includes 'municipal water utility' which universally has multiple intake/discharge points.

Step 8 (SAGE CONFIG) is a prerequisite for Step 9 (DATABASE DESIGN). This is backwards. Database schema should be designed based on domain requirements, not gated behind a YAML configuration step. The schema design for a time-series IoT system is a technical deliverable that should be independent of the agent framework configuration.

NTP time synchronization and clock drift are not addressed. Accurate timestamps are essential for EPA compliance (readings must have legally defensible timestamps). The firmware spec does not include NTP sync task, clock drift compensation, or handling of readings with invalid system time (e.g., before first NTP sync after power-on). A reading with a wrong timestamp in an EPA report is a compliance violation.
suggestions: Add a 5th sensor: Atlas Scientific EZO-RTD (temperature probe). Make temperature compensation a firmware-level requirement, not an optional calibration step. Update schema to include temperature_c column in all readings. All 3 EZO circuits (pH, DO, EC) expose temperature compensation commands — wire them together in the HAL.

Replace 'RDS_TimescaleDB' with an explicit choice: Timescale Cloud (managed, simplest), self-hosted TimescaleDB on EC2 within the same VPC (full control), or MSB. Document the chosen option in the infra architecture. If cost is a constraint, Timescale Cloud's compression reduces storage cost ~94% vs raw PostgreSQL.

Add an intermediate message bus (Amazon Kinesis Data Streams or MSK/Kafka) between EMQX and the TimescaleDB writer. This gives you replay-on-failure, back-pressure handling, and the ability to fan out the same stream to multiple consumers (ML anomaly detection, alert engine, archival) without coupling them to EMQX.

Replace the DFRobot SEN0189 with Atlas Scientific EZO-TBD + probe in the BOM and firmware spec. This keeps the sensor interface consistent (all EZO UART protocol) and provides a NIST-traceable calibration path required for EPA Method 180.1.

Add CROMERR compliance as a dedicated task between Steps 6 and 7. Define the electronic signature method (Level 2 e-signature for most NPDES submissions), identity proofing workflow for compliance officers, and the CDX (Central Data Exchange) submission format for federal EPA reporting.

Add MQTT QoS level as an explicit firmware and broker configuration requirement. Set QoS 1 for all sensor readings and QoS 2 for calibration records and alert events. Update EMQX configuration to enable persistent sessions and offline message queuing.

Add reconnect jitter and replay rate limiting to the firmware. On reconnect, each node should wait a random backoff (0–300s based on node_id hash) before replaying buffered readings, capped at a configurable max replay rate (e.g., 10 readings/second). Prevents thundering herd after mass outages.

Design the PKI and provisioning flow in Step 5 with specificity: define the CA hierarchy, the manufacturing-time provisioning flow (or first-boot provisioning via a claim certificate), and the cert rotation procedure. AWS IoT Core + Just-In-Time Provisioning would cover most of this without building custom PKI.

Add data_quality_flag (VARCHAR or ENUM) and is_temperature_compensated (BOOLEAN) columns to the readings table in Step 9. Add a view or materialized view that filters out unqualified readings for EPA report generation.

Move multi-site support to MVP scope for the schema and device identity layers only (add site_id foreign key to sensor_nodes, scope all queries by site_id). Defer the multi-site UI management to V2 as planned, but do not defer the data model change.
missing: Temperature sensor (EZO-RTD or DS18B20) in hardware BOM and temperature compensation logic in all sensor HAL drivers
CROMERR compliance design for electronic EPA submissions (40 CFR Part 3)
NPDES permit-specific report format support — most dischargers submit to state agencies via EDI or agency-specific portals, not generic PDF
PKI hierarchy design and zero-touch device provisioning workflow at manufacturing scale
Calibration interval management: scheduling, reminders, drift detection, and out-of-calibration data quarantine
Data quality flag column in schema and qualifier logic in EPA report generation
Durable message queue between EMQX and TimescaleDB writer (currently a reliability gap under the 99.5% uptime SLA)
Reconnect backoff and replay rate-limiting strategy for the 86,400-reading offline buffer
NTP sync task in firmware and timestamp validation before readings are committed to the queue
MQTT QoS level specified in firmware requirements and EMQX broker configuration
Firmware signing key management (HSM or AWS KMS for private key storage — cannot be a plaintext file in CI)
Certificate revocation mechanism (OCSP/CRL) for compromised or decommissioned devices
Sensor detection limit handling: what happens when DO = 0 or pH reads outside physical range? Schema and report generation must handle below-detection-limit values with appropriate flags
Multi-site data model (site_id) in MVP schema — cannot be retrofitted cleanly after launch
security_risks: Firmware signing key management unspecified. If the signing private key is stored in GitHub Actions secrets or as a plaintext CI environment variable, it can be exfiltrated via a compromised dependency or Actions runner. Require HSM or AWS KMS-backed signing with audit logging for every firmware release signature.

Zero-touch device provisioning gap creates a supply chain attack surface. Without a defined provisioning flow, someone inserting a rogue node with a valid claim certificate (or exploiting a permissive provisioning policy) could inject false readings into EPA compliance reports. Require per-device unique claim certificates burned at manufacturing, not shared claim certs.

EMQX topic authorization not specified. By default, EMQX permits any authenticated device to publish to any topic. A compromised sensor node could publish to other nodes' topics, injecting false readings. Require per-device ACL rules: node_id X can only publish to `sensors/{node_id}/readings`.

No mention of MQTT payload schema validation at the ingestion layer. A compromised device sending malformed JSON or oversize payloads could crash the FastAPI ingestion service or cause database errors. Validate payload schema and enforce max size at the EMQX hook layer before reaching the ingestion pipeline.

OAuth2 JWT secret rotation not addressed. If the JWT signing secret is static and leaked, all sessions are compromised with no recovery path. Require JWKS endpoint with short-lived tokens (15 min access, 24h refresh) and documented rotation procedure.

IEC 62443 zone model places MQTT broker in the OT zone with 'no direct OT→internet path', but sensors must reach the MQTT broker over the internet (or cellular) from field deployments. The zone model as described does not account for remote field devices — the OT zone would need to be a VPN tunnel termination, not a network segment. This misapplication of the zone model leaves field devices uncategorized.

No mention of rate limiting or anomaly detection on the MQTT ingestion path. A compromised node (or botnet of nodes) could flood the ingestion pipeline with high-frequency readings. Require per-device message rate limits enforced at the EMQX broker level.

SOC 2 Type II is listed as a compliance target but the timeline implies it would be in-scope from launch. SOC 2 Type II requires a minimum 6-month observation period of controls in operation. Either the launch date expectations are wrong, or the plan should target SOC 2 Type I for initial certification and Type II at the 12-month mark.
history.summary: This is an architecturally ambitious and well-structured plan that demonstrates genuine domain knowledge — the sensor selection, SAGE framework integration, IEC 62443 zoning intent, and TimescaleDB choice are all defensible. However, it has three categories of failure that would each independently block production: (1) Scientific invalidity — missing temperature compensation invalidates every pH, DO, and conductivity reading for EPA purposes, and the DFRobot turbidity sensor cannot satisfy Method 180.1, making the entire compliance reporting feature legally unusable; (2) Infrastructure assumptions that are wrong — TimescaleDB on RDS as described does not exist in the form assumed, and the absence of a durable message queue between EMQX and the DB writer means data loss is guaranteed under any ingestion service restart; (3) Compliance gaps — CROMERR is the legal framework for electronic EPA submissions and its absence means the product cannot file reports to federal or state agency data systems regardless of how correct the PDF looks. The plan would work as a prototype for demonstrating the architecture, but shipping it as a compliance product without addressing these issues would expose customers to regulatory liability. Score reflects strong structural foundation undermined by fundamental measurement, infrastructure, and regulatory gaps that require rework before production readiness.
